item.title: Feels weird to proud of this area
item.description:
https://www.nytimes.com/2025/05/07/us/salt-lake-city-boise-pride-flags.html?smid=nytcore-android-share
https://apnews.com/article/boise-salt-lake-city-pride-flags-law-13c8f9f7269d1b33daaa7ef041c6c497 https://www.ktvb.com/article/news/local/bosie-city-council-approves-flag-resolution-adopting-pride-flag-official-city-flag/277-1b75bb76-a807-4c7a-b862-b2ddb7c9aae1
But gosh darn it i might even come back home to visit yall now. And I WILL be bumpin Dixon Dallas across the Magic Valley, they can't stop me. We meet sunrise at Java! This chick wants her bowl o' soul

item.title: Hands On Technique Of Ma Jinfu
item.description:
Ma Jinfu's hands. (Photo/Wen Sirui)
Ma Jinfu shows some of his glass paintings in Xining, capital of northwest China's Qinghai Province. Song used: Francis Lai's OST of this movie: The Private Lesson (1968) #skills #awesome #art #aww #creativity

item.title: Art And Street Art Of Spanish Artist Pejac
item.description: Pejac was born in Santander (Cantabria, Spain) in 1977. His birth name is Silvestre Santiago. #skills #awesome #art #aww #creativity

item.dc:creator: ewakemanVA

item.title: This might be the saddest time of my life.
item.description:
I am completely heartbroken it has come to this end after so long. For so much of the relationship she was a very different person, but an autoimmune disorder and then alcoholism turned her into the farthest person from whom she once was. Last weekend she got her Second DUI in 7 years. in between those she has stolen from friends, family, and myself to feed her habit. She had become violent when drunk and would drink until she blacked out and collapsed. I had taken her to the hospital for help a number of times but it never stuck, she doesn't want help. Once in therapy years ago she told my therapist she would choose booze over me, I never wanted to believe it but it has become so painfully true. Her behavior has alienated all of her oldest and most dear friends & family as well as becoming absolutely incapable of being a trusted person. 50% chance I might be able to keep my house and car in the divorce but if not I start over from the ground up. I just know I can't go on watching her do this to herself anymore. thanks for taking the time to read my little venting rant. much peace to all you and your loved ones.
Here, have an empty broken hearted meme.
item.dc:creator: AdrienMenges

item.title: I've got Etch A Sketch hair now! <3
item.description:
Isn't it peachy keen!??! It's my first time with a crazy hair color so I thought I'd play 4D chess and just dye the bottom because I'm terrified it's gonna cost me at least tree fiddy to constantly retouch my roots!
I feel like I'm *this* much closer to achieving my dream of becoming an Actual Cartoon Character/Pokemon Gym Leader. Stay tuned for an unrelated video I'm working on that may or may not post today (likely tomorrow)!
item.dc:creator: pikajane

item.title: Beware the Maelstrom
item.description: Englishpluspodcast.com: A maelstrom is a tremendously powerful whirlpool. These natural phenomena form when powerful currents collide or rush through narrow channels, creating a relentless, swirling mass of water.

item.title: Phone-Clearing Dump 7 / ??
item.description:
My parents used to joke that I would grow up to be a parking lot valet because I didn't make my Matchbox cars go vroom-vroom, I made the cars line up neatly in rows.
Though, honestly, even if I'd taken the well-traveled road, I'd be lost and wondering why I was there in the first place
Dunno what is other than cute.
All memes returned here to use sub were originally pilfered to send to my gf. We exchange pebbles every night, my favorite ritual.
Fuckin' Gary, amiright?
It tastes like cough syrup, I love it!
Probably located in Boston
It's time to plant the planties again.
item.dc:creator: natangold

item.title: Clinton Briggs: Killed For Walking Down a Sidewalk
item.description:
Clinton Briggs, the son of Sandy and Catherine Briggs, was born on Friday, the 25th of November, 1892, although several records say December instead. He grew up in southern Arkansas, on a rented farm in Bartholomew Township, Lincoln County, about 18 miles southeast from the city of Pine Bluff. Clinton never had the chance to attend school, but his parents made certain that he could read and write. All told, Clinton lived what would have been a relatively normal childhood for a black boy in early 20th century Arkansas: He worked with his parents on the farm, and he did odd jobs here and there to help supplement the family income.
On June 5th, 1917, however, he registered for the draft as the United States formally entered into World War I and in June of the following year, 1918, he was called up for active service. Clinton trained at Camp Pike, and stayed there as part of the labor battalion helping to maintain and expand the base. He discharged in December of that year, and was awarded the World War I Victory Medal and the World War I Victory Lapel Button for his service. Clinton returned home to Lincoln County, took a job working for the planter who owned his childhood farm, and in March of 1919 he married a woman named Cora. What Clinton returned to, however, was an ever-so-slightly charged atmosphere. Something had changed in Lincoln County, and all over the South. Black men returning home from war or from camps, trained in combat, armed, and in uniform represented something many white people couldn't stomach. There had already been incidents of violence, such as the Bisbee Riot between returning black soldiers and local police in Arizona, or the New London Riots between returning black and white soldiers in Connecticut. A black man wearing his uniform in many parts of the South, in fact, was effectively an invitation to violence. Lynchings in the years following the war steadily trended upward as a system built on racial inequality and cheap labor clashed against returning veterans.
Clinton, too, found himself squarely in the cross-hairs of that change. The looks, the side-eyes, the increased scrutiny, it all added up until it burst one afternoon in Star City, in September, 1919. While walking down the sidewalk in town, Clinton moved out of the way of a white couple walking towards him. That was not, however, the correct and expected act, and the woman in the couple brushed against Clinton in what was most likely an intentional snub. "N-----s get OFF the sidewalk down here," she informed him in a scolding tone. Clinton, though, held his head high and replied with "This a free man's county." This was taken as an insult, and the woman's companion immediately rounded on Clinton and grabbed him. He was quickly joined by other white men, and under the claim that Clinton had insolently insulted a white woman they strong-armed him into a vehicle belonging to one of the men.
They drove Clinton to a farm somewhere nearby, chained him to a tree, and tortured him before they shot him to death. According to stories passed down in the Briggs family, the men castrated Clinton before they murdered him. His body wasn't found until days later. Clinton Biggs was murdered for walking down a sidewalk. He's buried in the Bethlehem #2 (African American) Cemetery, at the end of a lonely road about seven miles north of Star City. His story is one that was common all throughout the region in the years following World War I, and it's a story that was repeated in the years following World War II. I'm sorry that I couldn't find any photos of Clinton Briggs, but I felt his story was a good representation of what was going on in the era.
item.dc:creator: Luriden
